programs.name: Aged and Disabled HCBS Waiver
programs.what_it_does: Provides Medicaid-funded home and community-based services to prevent institutionalization for seniors and disabled individuals. Services include adult day health care, assisted living, assistive technology, home modifications, personal care, companionship, home-delivered meals, and respite.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Individuals aged 65+ or disabled at risk of nursing home placement due to limited ability to function independently; over half of participants are 65 and older.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by assessed needs; coordinates long-term care services at home.[1]

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.what_it_does: Not explicitly named or detailed in Nebraska sources as actively administered; federal PACE provides comprehensive medical and social services. Nebraska may participate via DHHS coordination, but no state-specific name found.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Nursing home eligible seniors 55+ who can live safely in community with services.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Full capitation coverage; limited or no active program confirmed in state plan.[1]

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Assists low-income Medicare beneficiaries with paying premiums, deductibles, and copayments via state subsidies for Parts A, B, and D. QMB covers full Part B premium and more; SLMB and QI provide partial assistance.[2][8]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors on Medicare; specific income/resources vary (e.g., tied to SSI limits for some).[2][8]
programs.benefit_value: QMB: Full Part B premium ($174.70/mo 2024) + deductibles; SLMB: Part B premium; QI: partial premium.[2]

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.what_it_does: Provides food benefits to low-income households via EBT card for grocery purchases. Administered federally but through Nebraska DHHS ACCESSNebraska.[7]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors, disabled, and families; qualifies for other programs like NTAP/Lifeline.[7]
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per household; varies by income/size.[7]

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.what_it_does: Not directly named in results but standard federal program administered via Nebraska Energy Office/DHHS for heating/cooling assistance. Helps with energy bills and crisis aid.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors 60+; priority for elderly/disabled.[3]
programs.benefit_value: $200-1,000 one-time grant per season; varies by need.[3]

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.what_it_does: Not explicitly detailed; federal DOE program administered via Nebraska Energy Office for home energy efficiency upgrades. Improves insulation, seals leaks for low-income homes.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and disabled; priority populations.[3]

programs.name: Nebraska SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free Medicare counseling, plan comparisons, and assistance with subsidies/Extra Help. Educates and empowers seniors on health insurance options.[8]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare beneficiaries, especially low-income seniors and guardians.[8]
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; helps secure $1,000s in annual savings.[8]

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals (via AAAs)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors, funded under Older Americans Act. Expanded during pandemics with grab-and-go options.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals.[3]

programs.name: Lifespan Respite Services
programs.what_it_does: Funds respite care for family caregivers via Nebraska Lifespan Respite Network; includes recruitment/screening of providers. Supports caregivers of seniors/disabled.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid primary family caregivers of eligible care recipients across lifespan.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Vouchers/purchased services; amount based on need.[1]

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Not directly named; federal OAA program typically run via AAAs for part-time paid training/work. Builds skills for unsubsidized employment.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage part-time work (20 hrs/wk) + training.[3]

programs.name: Legal Services for Seniors (via AAAs/OAA)
programs.what_it_does: Provides legal aid, consultation, and representation for seniors on issues like benefits, housing, abuse. Funded via Area Agencies on Aging.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Free/low-cost legal help; expanded hours via funding.[3]

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes/assisted living; investigates complaints, ensures quality care. Coordinated statewide via AAAs/DHHS.[1][3]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors in long-term care facilities and their families.[3]

programs.name: Nebraska Community Aging Services Act
programs.what_it_does: Provides state funding for in-home services through Area Agencies on Aging to help seniors remain in their homes. Coordinates care management for those who do not qualify for Medicaid-funded programs.[1][2]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults needing assistance to stay at home who do not qualify for Medicaid, family caregivers.[1][2]
programs.whats_unique: State-funded program beyond federal Older Americans Act, specifically funds care management and in-home services not covered by Medicaid.[1][2]
programs.benefit_value: Varies by assessed needs; funds services like care management, home-delivered meals, personal care.

programs.name: Aging and Disability Resource Center Act
programs.what_it_does: Funds Aging and Disability Resource Centers that offer information, referrals, and assistance for long-term care options. Helps connect seniors and disabled individuals to community services.[1][7]
programs.who_its_for: Nebraskans 60+, people with disabilities of all ages, family caregivers.[7]
programs.whats_unique: State-funded ADRCs providing centralized access to services, distinct from federal programs.[1][7]
programs.benefit_value: Free information, referrals, and options counseling; no direct monetary value.

programs.name: Social Services for the Aged and Disabled (SSAD)
programs.what_it_does: Provides state-funded social services to support aged and disabled individuals in maintaining independence. Includes assistance not covered by federal programs.[8]
programs.whats_unique: State-specific program listed separately from federal aid, targeted at seniors and disabled.[8]
programs.benefit_value: Varies; social services support, specific amounts not detailed in sources.

programs.name: Assistance to Aged, Blind or Disabled (AABD)
programs.what_it_does: Offers state assistance to aged, blind, or disabled individuals for basic needs. Complements federal programs with state resources.[8]
programs.who_its_for: Aged, blind, or disabled Nebraska residents.[8]
programs.whats_unique: Nebraska state program providing targeted aid beyond standard federal benefits.[8]
programs.benefit_value: Cash or service assistance; amounts based on eligibility and need.
